comments: Flashback '71 profiled the year with contemporary tracks and session repeats. There was no Friday Night Connection.
Tommy wishes us happy Easter (the programme went out on Good Friday), and claims it took six hours to put together.

sessions.details: Recorded 1971-06-29 (Bob Harris, Sounds Of The Seventies). Available on Barclay James Harvest And Other Short Stories (remastered reissue) (EMI Music Japan).
Recorded 1971-04-27 for Top Gear (Peel Session #4). 'Stone-Hearted Mama' and 'Cold Stone' available on The BBC Sessions (Repertoire).
Recorded 1971-10-20 (Pete Drummond, Sounds Of the Seventies). All except 'July Morning' available on Look At Yourself (remastered reissue) (BMG Japan).
Recorded 1971-04-21 (Stuart Henry, Sounds Of The Seventies). No known commercial release.

track_listing.details: LP-X In Search Of Space; United Artists

track_listing.track: 'Rock N' Roll Children (LP-One Way Or Another)' (ATCO)
'Someone There You Know' (Harris Session) Ken Garner's In Session Tonight (p. 216) does not list this track. However, the official BJH site claims it was not TX on the first transmission.
'Stay With Me (LP-A Nod's As Good As A Wink...To A Blind Horse)' (Warner Bros.)

'Jailbait' (? Session) This was recorded in session for both Bob Harris and John Peel in this year, so it is not possible at this stage to identify which recording it is, even though Tommy gives the impression both tracks were in the same one.

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Happy Easter. Well hello there. This is TV on the radio, Thomas the Vance.
And welcome to the Friday Rock Show.
Right, tonight something that pops up every once in a while in the Friday Rock Show.
This Good Friday we have Flashback 71.
During the next two hours you'll hear just some of the great music that ten years ago entertained, stimulated and pleased.
We've got some BBC archive sessions by Barclay James Harvest, by The Pretty Things, by Uriah Heep, and also by Wishbone Ash.
Classic tracks from The Doors, Black Sabbath, Yes, Genesis, Deep Purple, The Faces, Cactus, The Rolling Stones, and others.
But this evening, we start with this.
Who really deserved a lot more attention, I think, than they ever got.
I remember the last time I went to see Jeff Beck, which was the first time for about ten years,
I was talking to one of the guys out of Samson, and he said to me,
do us a favour, one of these days, dig out an album by Cactus
and play a track of that particular band on the air.
And we've just done that from a 1971 album that they put out
about ten years ago.
The album was called One Way or Another.
Carmen Apeachy and Tim Bogart and Rusty Day actually was the vocalist.
Apeachy and Bogart used to be in the Vanilla Fudge
and Rusty Day was in Mitch Ryder and the Detroit Wheels.
And they really were a tasty, tasty band.
Before that, you heard something which has got to be
the most voted-for track by Hawkwind from 1971.
It was from their second album.

but we thought it sort of indicated
exactly where the doors were 10 years ago
way back in 1971.
